ocr_text:
Technology Briefings
INTERNET ELECTION.COM TO RUN ICANN VOTING The nonprofit agency charged with administering Internet domain names and technical functions said yesterday that online balloting for its board members would be handled by Election.com . More than 76,000 Internet users worldwide have registered to participate in the vote for five board members of the Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers. The election, to be held from Oct. 1 to Oct. 10, will seat 5 of the group's 19 board members. Nine are named by advisory committees and four more will be elected by Internet users in the future.

TECHNOLOGY BRIEFING: INTERNET; ELECTION.COM TO RUN ICANN VOTING
Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers, nonprofit agency charged with administering Internet domain names and technical functions, says online balloting for its board members will be handled by Election.com; more than 76,000 Internet users worldwide have registered to participate in vote for five board members to be held from Oct 1 to Oct 10 (S)

THE 2000 CAMPAIGN: THE STRATEGIES; Democrats Remind Gore of the Economy
Prominent Democrats, frustrated and worried that Vice Pres Al Gore has failed to capitalize on what they see as his best selling point, say he must devote closing week of presidential campaign delivering urgent pitch about his role in Clinton administration's economic successes; several, including Gore's most enthusiastic supporters, are nearly unanimous in belief that he has not benefitted politically from flourishing economy; say best way to overtake Bush is to convince voters he has record and skill to keep economy strong (M)

CAMPAIGN LAUGH TRACK
Table of most joked-about politicians in late-night network TV monologues; samples of jokes

THE MARKETS: STOCKS; Drug Shares Advance While Major Indexes End Mixed
Dow Jones industrial average gains 159.26, or 1.5 percent, to 10,977.21; Nasdaq composite index falls 35.57, or 1 percent, to 3,416.21; Standard & Poor's 500 stock index rises 5.50 points, or 0.4 percent, to 1,432.19; graph; chart (M)
CRITIC'S NOTEBOOK; Can Art Cinema Survive Cruder Times?

Covering Prescription Drugs
Editorial assesses prescription-drug proposals; says need for coverage is undeniable, but that any new benefit will carry substantial price tag; notes that George W Bush seems to be leaning toward private-market model for Medicare drug coverage, while Al Gore would fund new public program

Discarded Hindu God Does Not Bless India's Water
Bombay's annual Ganesh Festival begins, 10-day celebration of Hindu god Ganesh--bestower of happiness and eliminator of sorrow; festival is as much partying as prayer; tens of thousands of brightly colored statues of long-trunked deity are carried through the streets, then put in honored places in private homes or elaborate outdoor shrines; environmentalists do not like the way event traditionally ends, with icons disposed of in ceremonial immersion, by leaving them to disintegrate in lakes, creeks and conveniently located Arabian Sea; environmentalists contend there are massive fish deaths from toxins in paint; photos (L)

Taking a Closer Look At the Peril of Plum Island
Rev Judy Fine letter on August 13 article on Plum Island tests of 'mad' sheep suggests Long Island facility be shut down (S)

OLYMPICS; For a Japanese Gymnast, Honor Thy Father
Profile of Naoya Tsukahara, Japanese gynmast who will attempt to win gold medal in Summer Olympics in Sydney, Australia, by performing difficult 'moon' somersault devised by father Mitsuo Tsukahara, who used it in Munich in 1972 to win his first individual gold medal on horizontal bar; somersault, which revolutionized event of vaulting for men and women, consists of cartwheel with quarter-turn followed by back somersault; photos; diagram (M)

COVER STORY; Making More Air Time for Water Polo, Archery, Team Handball and Canoeing
CNBC and MSNBC plan to render broad coverage of variety of sporting events at Summer, 2000, Olympic Games; photos (M)

FOR THE RECORD; In Water and on Land, Women Hope for Medals
Profiles of Olympic swimming gold medal winner Cristina Teuscher and Sue Blinks, leading American rider in dressage; two Westchester County, NY, residents will compete in 2000 Summer Olympics; photos (M)

OLYMPICS: NOTEBOOK; The Flame and the Flag Come Into Play
Australian athletes speculate on who will carry Australian flag during opening ceremonies for 2000 Summer Games and who will light Olympic caldron; photo (M)
MERTO BRIEFING

2000 SYDNEY GAMES/TV Sports; Live from Sydney, It's CBC. Taped and Packaged, It's NBC.
Richard Sandomir article on Sydney Olympics television coverage notes that all of NBC's coverage will be taped, while Canadian Broadcasting Corp will carry everything live; planned coverage on CNBC and MSNBC discussed (M)

U.S. OPEN: NOTEBOOK; Dokic Looks Within in a Comeback Victory
Jelena Dokic defeats Francesca Schiavone in third round of US Open (S)

New Economy; Whose name is it anyway? Arbitration panels favoring trademark holders in disputes over Web names.
Arbitration panels are favoring trademark holders in disputes over Web names; Andrew McLaughlin, president of Internet Corp for Assigned Names and Numbers, international nonprofit group that administers domain-name system, defends arbitration process, which started in December 1999 as way of giving trademark holders easy way to resolve growing number of battles over names; drawing (M)
WORLD BRIEFING

OLYMPICS: NOTEBOOK; American Basketball Teams Check Out
Olympics Notebook article focuses on United States men's and women's basketball teams, which vacated their Melbourne team hotel and arrived in Sydney after unrelated protest outside hotel disrupted their practice schedules; Chinese officials admit nearly all of 27 athletes cut from Olympic team failed drug tests, mostly for performance-enhancing drug EPO; holds coach Ma Junren was responsible for six runners who failed tests; holds EPO has polluted world sports stage (M)

Games Are Nearly Here
SYDNEY, Australia, Friday, Sept. 15 — A beleaguered Olympic movement sought to sustain its viability and to shift its focus toward the possibilities of international brotherhood and the achievements of the world's greatest athletes as the 27th Summer Games open today in what is late winter in the Southern Hemisphere.

Games Are Nearly Here
SYDNEY, Australia, Friday, Sept. 15 — A beleaguered Olympic movement sought to sustain its viability and to shift its focus toward the possibilities of international brotherhood and the achievements of the world's greatest athletes as the 27th Summer Games open today in what is late winter in the Southern Hemisphere.

SYDNEY 2000: THE OVERVIEW; The Games Are Finally Here, Even if They Are Over There
27th Summer Olympic Games open, Sydney, Australia; 10,500 athletes from 199 nations prepare to march in opening ceremonies; Australia fields largest team, with 628 athletes; torch relay has been exceedingly popular in Australia, giving ordinary people link to heavily commercialized Olympics; photos (M)

As Electoral Vote Takes Shape, Campaigns Focus on Perceptions
WASHINGTON, Sept. 16 — Vice President Al Gore, who has erased Gov. George W. Bush's lead in most national polls, has also edged ahead of him in the battle for electoral votes, according to independent and partisan analysts. But despite the postconvention trend in Mr. Gore's favor, enough states are either tossups or held so narrowly that the race remains fiercely competitive.

THE 2000 CAMPAIGN: THE NUMBERS; Gun Plan Shows Bush Isn't Solid in Colorado
Strong support in Colorado for ballot initiative to require background checks of those buying guns at shows is among early signs that Gov George W Bush may not win state; latest poll shows Vice Pres Al Gore has erased Bush's big lead; Democrats are investing more capital in state, with visit by Sen Joseph I Lieberman to be followed by visit by Gore's daughter, Kristen, and possible Gore visit (M)

THE 2000 CAMPAIGN: THE VOTERS; Bucking the Swing-State Trend, Ohio Holding Steady, Polls Show
Battlegrounds, second article in series on four states likely to be critical to outcome of presidential race, finds Gov George W Bush holding reasonably steady lead in Ohio, bucking trend in which Pennsylvania and several other swing states may have shifted toward Vice Pres Al Gore; polls indicate candidates have both built solid partisan bases within state, considered political microcosm since Civil War, and are fighting over perhaps 5 to 6 percent of electorate, composed largely of independents, suburban residents and women; maps (M)

The Stiff Guy vs. the Dumb Guy
Marshall Sella article on lampooning of Vice Pres Al Gore and Gov George W Bush by late-night television talk show hosts, who are becoming less impartial and more influential with voters; photos (L)

Why Polls Are Fickle
CHICAGO — One week Al Gore leads in the polls, the next week George W. Bush does, and the week after that they switch again. Or in the same week, one major pollster picks Mr. Gore and another picks Mr. Bush. It may be a close race, but it is doubtful that the voters are really as capricious as they have seemed this fall. What may be skewing our picture of their preferences are the questions the polls are asking.

Political Briefing; Tough Ad Campaign Takes on the Favorite
Washington Gov Gary Locke, Democrat, is favored to win re-election to second term this fall, but Republicans have not surrendered yet, and are broadcasting tough advertisements designed to give Republican primary winner a quick boost; photo (S)

Weather May Slow Track Events
Popular in Europe but largely off the American radar screen between Olympic years, track and field continues to be the flagship sport of the Games. This year there is added spice as Marion Jones attempts to become the first woman in track and field to win five gold medals in one Olympics. Cold, windy weather may prevent any world records.

2000 SYDNEY GAMES; Track and Field
Preview of Sydney Olympics track and field events; photo (M)

OLYMPICS; Witty Chasing a Special Double
Profile of Christine Witty, speed skater who will compete for United States at Sydney Games in bicycling in bid to become only fourth person ever to win medals in both Winter and Summer Olympics; she comments, interview; photos (M)

THE 2000 CAMPAIGN: THE VICE PRESIDENT; Democrats Raise Money; Republicans Make Hay
Vice Pres Al Gore and his running mate, Sen Joseph I Lieberman, headline star-spangled musical benefit for their party at Radio City Music Hall in New York City; some of popular music's best-known names attend, highlighting spree of copious fund-raising that Republicans say undercut this week's Democratic crusade against entertainment industry's marketing practices; photo (M)

HOCKEY; Isles' Top Draft Choice To Miss 7 to 10 Days
New York I

A Witness From Australia's 'Stolen Generation'
In 1942, when John Kundereri Moriarty was 4 years old and living happily in an Aboriginal tribal community in northern Australia, he was taken from his family. It happened like a kidnapping, he recalled in an interview. "My mum went to pick me up fr

An Array of Stars Is in Store for Week One
SATURDAY, SEPT. 16. Burning question: By how many body lengths will Ian Thorpe win the men's 400-meter freestyle? .Other diversions: Sydney organizers decided to follow the Olympic trend for urban renewal by turning a toxic-waste dump at Homebush Bay in

Thorpe´s Flame Burns Brightly in the Water
SYDNEY, Australia, Sept. 16 -- There are powerful images that can be fleeting, and those that are unforgettable and real. One night after choreographing a utopian multiculturalism that smacked of fiction, the host Australians trotted out the real deal for the world to behold.

SYDNEY 2000: SWIMMING -- Sports of The Times; Thorpe's Flame Burns in the Water
Harvey Araton Sports of The Times column discusses Australian swimmer Ian Thorpe, who has won two gold medals; touts his magnetic presence and smooth, technically superior swimming style; photo (M)

Retailers Post Weak August, Hoping It's Not Holiday Omen
Goldman Sachs retail composite index shows retail sales for August in stores open at least a year rose just 3 percent, lower than analysts expected and weakest August results in three years; results for specific major retailers noted; graph; chart (M)

Newly Certified Teachers, Looking for a Job, Find a Paradox
Dozens of new certified teachers in New York City find their job searches thrown into chaos by court order issued last week that compels Board of Education to assign all newly hired certified teachers to city's 94 lowest-performing schools until 400 vacancies there are filled; in paradox, new teachers find that having certification is barrier to getting into school of their choice, and that being uncertified would have made their search easier; rather than work in failing school, some are choosing to seek jobs outside city; photos (M)

Use of Illegal Drugs Is Down Among Young, Survey Finds
National Household Survey on Drug Abuse finds that use of illegal drugs by youths aged 12 to 17 dropped sharply from 1997 to 1999; Sec Donna Shalala and Gen Barry McCaffrey credit preventive efforts; photo; graph; study finds 21 percent drop among young people 12 to 17, and 21 percent drop in marijuana use specifically; use of illicit drugs by people 18 to 25 rose 28 percent, with 18.8 percent reporting use of illicit drugs during period (S)

6 Real Estate Companies Submit Bids on 99-Year Lease for the World Trade Center
Six companies submit bids to take over World Trade Center under 99-year lease; officials of Port Authority of New York and New Jersey, which owns trade center, will not disclose who made bids to take buildings private or value of proposals; one critical feature of any deal would be end of arrangement under which Port Authority pays New York City $27.5 million a year in lieu of real estate taxes on trade center; city officials say successful bidder would have to pay full taxes, about $100 million a year (M)

A Global Warming Report Predicts Doom for Many Species
World Wide Fund for Nature (World Wildlife Fund) reports that global warming could wipe out many species of plants and animals by end of 21st century; predicts devastation for species from Arctic polar bears to New England sugar maples (S)

Hiring of Teachers Is More Than a Matter of Decree
News analysis: New York State decision to direct most qualified teachers who are hired this fall by New York City to system's worst schools is having unintended consequences; as teachers seeking jobs have been offered chance to work in failing schools or nothing, many of them have simply withdrawn, if only temporarily, from job market; city officials predict state policy will hurt marginal schools (M)

NATO Hails 'Prudent' Course, And Putin Sees Added Stability
The North Atlantic Treaty Organization on Friday welcomed President Bill Clinton's decision to defer the deployment of a National Missile Defense system, and diplomats said the president's announcement would be greeted with relief by U.S. allies. .The

The Jewish Wars
Stephen J Whitfield reviews book Jew vs Jew: The Struggle for the Soul of American Jewry by Samuel G Freedman (M)

A.F.C. Scouting Reports
American Football Conference scouting reports, team by team; teams are listed in predicted order of finish; photos (M)

THEATER; The Bard of American Politics, Still Campaigning
Profile of playwright Gore Vidal in light of return to Broadway of his 1959 play The Best Man, starring Charles Durning, Spalding Gray and Elisabeth Ashley and directed by Ethan McSweeny; photo (M)

SYDNEY 2000: CRITIC'S NOTEBOOK; High Camp And the Games' Cookie Cutter
Critics Notebook column discusses NBC coverage of Sydney Olympics; describes it as overproduced, misbegotten mess, with too many sentimental features and not enough real sports events (M)
NEWS SUMMARY

Company Challenges Law Banning Net Tobacco Sales
In the latest of a flurry of lawsuits seeking to use the federal Constitution to trump state efforts to regulate controversial online services and products, a major tobacco company has challenged a recently-enacted New York law that effectively bans the sale of cigarettes to New York consumers via the Internet, mail-order or telephone.

Company Challenges Law Banning Net Tobacco Sales
In the latest of a flurry of lawsuits seeking to use the federal Constitution to trump state efforts to regulate controversial online services and products, a major tobacco company has challenged a recently-enacted New York law that effectively bans the sale of cigarettes to New York consumers via the Internet, mail-order or telephone.
In Lean Times for Chinese Art, Dealers Triumph Over Auctioneers

In Lean Times for Chinese Art, Dealers Triumph Over Auctioneers
Last September, a marked shift in the balance of commercial power between auction houses and the art trade could be observed in the international scene within a specific area that has never had it so good — Chinese art.. If it attracted little atte

THE 2000 CAMPAIGN: THE PRESIDENT; Gore Puts Limit On Politicking By the President
Gore campaign tells Pres Clinton to steer clear of closely contested states like Michigan and Pennsylvania to avoid alienating swing voters who will play crucial role in choosing his successor; tells Clinton to concentrate on efforts to motivate black and Hispanic voters to get to polls, and to limit his travel to states that Gore is likely to win; polls show that while most Americans generally approve of Clinton's performance as president, his personal appeal among them is low; photo (M)

THE 2000 CAMPAIGN: THE SENATE; Democrats Given a Shot At Regaining a Majority
Democrats appear to have chance of regaining control of Senate as several races tighten; analysis of contests in key states; photos (M)
